perturbation.original_text: 1.This Agreement will apply to any disclosure of Information on the Effective Date and for [12] months thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.This Agreement will apply to any disclosure of Information on the Effective Date and for [6] months thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the agreement duration to 6 months could be problematic if trade secrets are involved and require longer protection under applicable laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets (potentially Uniform Trade Secrets Act, adopted by many US states, or equivalent laws in other jurisdictions, such as the UK)
perturbation.law_citation: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings to protect trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act outlines protections for trade secrets, and a 6-month period might be insufficient to protect valuable trade secrets, potentially undermining the purpose of the law. Courts often consider the 'commercial reasonableness' of the duration of confidentiality, and 6 months could be deemed insufficient depending on the industry and nature of the information.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract duration, reduced from 12 to 6 months, presents a potential contradiction under the Defend Trade Secrets Act. The law explanation suggests that a 6-month protection period for trade secrets might be deemed insufficient, failing to meet the 'commercial reasonableness' standard often considered by courts. While the provided law snippet (18 USC 1836) primarily focuses on civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, its existence implies that trade secrets are legally protected. Therefore, reducing the protection period in the contract could undermine the intended legal safeguards for trade secrets, potentially contradicting the spirit and purpose of trade secret law. The change is inconsistent with ensuring reasonable protection, thus creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation also highlights this concern.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall keep confidential the Information for [five (5)] years from the date of disclosure, and shall not disclose the Information to any other person or company without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent, and shall not itself make any use of such Information for any purpose other than the purpose of supply of logistics services ("the Authorised Purpose").
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall keep confidential the Information for [one (1)] year from the date of disclosure, and shall not disclose the Information to any other person or company without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent, and shall not itself make any use of such Information for any purpose other than the purpose of supply of logistics services ("the Authorised Purpose").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect certain types of confidential information, particularly trade secrets, thus contradicting laws designed to protect them.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets (potentially Uniform Trade Secrets Act, adopted by many US states, or equivalent laws in other jurisdictions, such as the UK)
perturbation.law_citation: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings to protect trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secret law provides indefinite protection as long as the information remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage. Limiting the confidentiality to one year could be considered unreasonable and insufficient to protect the trade secret, violating the spirit and intent of trade secret laws. Additionally, given this agreement is governed under the laws of England, limiting the confidentiality obligations in a way that undermines the protection of trade secrets may conflict with English common law principles regarding confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The law explanation and perturbation explanation suggest that this may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially violating the intent of trade secret laws that provide indefinite protection as long as the information remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, outlines civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets, indicating that misappropriation can lead to civil action. Reducing the confidentiality period could weaken the protection of trade secrets, making it more difficult to pursue legal action against potential violations, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: forthwith upon receipt of a written request from the Disclosing Party or upon termination: (i) return all information supplied by the Disclosing Party as well as items and materials relating to or derived from the Information; (ii) deliver to the Disclosing Party or at its request destroy immediately all items and materials made by the Receiving Party containing Information, that are not returned pursuant to paragraph (i) above; (iii) not keep copies or duplicates of any items or materials referred to in paragraphs (i) or (ii) above; and (iv) provide a certificate signed by a senior officer of the Receiving Party confirming that the provisions of this clause have been complied with.
perturbation.changed_text: forthwith upon receipt of a written request from the Disclosing Party or upon termination: (i) return all information supplied by the Disclosing Party as well as items and materials relating to or derived from the Information; (ii) deliver to the Disclosing Party or at its request destroy items and materials made by the Receiving Party containing Information, that are not returned pursuant to paragraph (i) above after a period of 180 days; (iii) keep copies or duplicates of any items or materials referred to in paragraphs (i) or (ii) above; and (iv) provide a certificate signed by a junior officer of the Receiving Party confirming that the provisions of this clause have been complied with.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the destruction timeline and allowing copies to be kept may violate laws protecting trade secrets or confidential data, especially if retention periods are mandated for compliance purposes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) - Data Minimization and Storage Limitation Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(e) of the GDPR states that personal data shall be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed. Allowing copies and delaying destruction for 180 days after termination may violate this principle if the information contains personal data and the retention is not justified by a legitimate purpose.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’).
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts GDPR principles related to data retention. Specifically, Article 5(1)(e) of the GDPR, as quoted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that personal data should be 'kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed'. The changed text allows the Receiving Party to keep copies of information and delays destruction for 180 days, which directly clashes with the 'storage limitation' principle outlined in the GDPR. This is because retaining data for an extended period without a legitimate purpose violates the GDPR's requirement to minimize data retention.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of England and the parties submit to the nonexclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of Nevada and the parties submit to the nonexclusive jurisdiction of the English Courts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada while retaining jurisdiction in English courts creates a conflict of law issue, potentially violating principles of legal certainty and enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Conflict of Laws Principles (Generally Accepted Legal Principles)
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally agree on governing law, maintaining jurisdiction in a foreign court while applying a different law (Nevada Law in English Courts) creates a conflict of laws issue. Courts typically consider the chosen law's relationship to the transaction and parties. Enforcing Nevada law in English courts could be problematic if Nevada law conflicts with fundamental English legal principles or public policy. This undermines legal certainty and predictability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by stipulating that Nevada law governs the agreement while simultaneously maintaining jurisdiction in English courts. This creates a conflict of laws issue. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' By choosing Nevada law but litigating in English courts, the agreement sets up a scenario where the English court must interpret and apply Nevada law, potentially leading to legal uncertainty and undermining the principle of legal certainty and predictability, as stated in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will not be assignable or transferable by either party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be assignable or transferable by either party with verbal consent.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing assignment with only verbal consent contradicts the Statute of Frauds which, in many jurisdictions, requires certain contracts (including those involving significant intellectual property) to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_citation: English Law, Law of Property Act 1925, Section 53
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 53 of the Law of Property Act 1925 requires certain instruments, including assignments of equitable interests (which can include intellectual property rights associated with confidential information), to be in writing. Allowing assignment with only verbal consent contradicts this requirement, potentially rendering the assignment unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by allowing assignment or transfer of the agreement with only verbal consent. This contradicts the Statute of Frauds, which, as indicated by the Law Explanation, often requires certain contracts (including those related to intellectual property) to be in writing to be enforceable. Although the snippets do not explicitly define what the Statute of Frauds entails, the Law Explanation states that the Statute of Frauds typically mandates written agreements for specific types of contracts, this aligns with the Perturbation Explanation about contracts involving significant intellectual property.

perturbation.original_text: 7.Notices shall be delivered personally, or by prepaid first class mail, or transmitted by email to the parties at such address or number as the parties shall specify from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.Notices shall be delivered via carrier pigeon to the parties at such address or number as the parties shall specify from time to time.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting acceptable notice methods to carrier pigeon is insufficient and likely violates modern legal standards for providing reasonable notice, especially given electronic communication standards.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles - Sufficiency of Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: While not codified in a single statute, a fundamental principle of contract law is that notice must be reasonable and sufficient to inform the other party. Restricting notice to only carrier pigeon delivery is commercially unreasonable in the 21st century and unlikely to be considered sufficient, as it is unreliable, slow, and not generally accepted as a standard method of communication. This could lead to disputes over whether proper notice was given, rendering contractual provisions unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: notice | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you notice Notice is the constitutional requirement that a party being brought into court be informed of the controversy . Due Process clauses of the United States Constitution prohibit courts from hearing a case that could adversely affect a party's interest unless that party has been given proper notice. To satisfy this notice requirement, notice must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties that a lawsuit is pending and that it could adversely affect their interests. The degree of required notice varies depending on what type of jurisdiction a court intends to exercise. To exercise in personam jurisdiction, in-hand service of process is usually required. For in rem actions, which can affect the interests of anyone in the world, the plaintiff usually must inform all known parties of interest by a reasonably reliable means, and may then inform the rest of the world by "notice by publication" - purchasing a notice in a local newspaper multiple times over a period of several weeks. In tricky cases, courts work with plaintiffs to identify the best way of meeting notice requirements. In some unusual circumstances, a court may find traditional methods of notice are not satisfactory for a particular party. Notice is not a mere formality, as the Supreme Court recently discussed in Jones v. Flowers . In Jones v. Flowers , a state seized and sold property for unpaid property taxes. A certified letter meant to notify the owner of the impending sale was returned to the state as "unclaimed." Afterwards, the state made no additional attempt to contact the owner directly. The Court rejected the state's notice as insufficient, emphasizing that notice “must be such as one desirous of actually informing the absentee might reasonably adopt to accomplish it." Because of the constitutional importance of ensuring proper notice, courts will not forgive notice achieved through improper means even where a party actually learns of the controversy. [Last reviewed in October of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex CIVICS the Constitution THE LEGAL PROCESS wex articles civil procedure constitutional law courts and procedure individual rights Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility - Definition, Examples, Processes Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility March 29, 2015 by: Content Team When a parent or guardian caring for children requests, or is receiving, public assistance, the state requires that the other parent fulfill his or her financial responsibilities toward their biological children. Whether the state welfare agency receives a new application for such public assistance as food stamps, welfare benefits, or Medicaid benefits, or is being asked to enforce a current child support order, a Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility is legally served on the other biological parent. The form orders that parent to pay child support, and ensure the children are covered under his or her medical insurance. In the event the other parent is the father, and contests the issue of paternity , the form orders him to take a paternity test. To explore this concept, consider the following Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility definition. Definition of Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility Noun An official notice served to a non-custodial parent after the state assumes he or she should be financially responsible for a child. Child Support According to the laws in every state, both parents are required to be financially responsible for their children. Commonly, the non- custodial parent is ordered to pay a specific amount of child support to the custodial parent each month to help pay for the child’s needs. The amount of child support to be paid is set by the court, and cannot be waived by either parent, as this financial support is the right of the child , not the parent. In addition to monthly child support payments, the non-custodial parent is usually ordered to pay one-half of the child’s medical, dental, educational, daycare, and extracurricular activity expenses. In most cases, child support is terminated when the child reaches the age of majority, 18 in most states. Child support may, however, continue past the age of majority, if the child is attending college full time. If this is expected, it will be explicitly stated in the court order. Service of a Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility There are many circumstances in which a parent may be served with a Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility. These include: The state has made an order for child support The custodial parent requests or receives public assistance A request for support is made by a different state or federal agency Service of a Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility must be accomplished in the same what that a civil summons is served. The manner of service is defined in the laws and code of civil procedure of each state. In many states, service of a Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility can be made by an employee of the agency, through the use of certified mail, or other mail service with delivery confirmation. Other states require the Notice to be personally served by a process server, deputy sheriff, or constable. What is Included in the Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility While the laws of each state specify what must be included in the official Notice and Finding of Financial Responsibility, information commonly required in the Notice includes: Name of the child for whom support is to be paid Name and the child’s caretaker – this may be the custodial parent, a guardian, or an agency A statement of the monthly child support amount due A statement of the past due child support A notification that the parent may be required to provide health insurance coverage for the child Establishing Paternity In many cases, the paternity of the child for whom benefits have been requested has already been established. If paternity is in question, the Notice will provide information about the mother’s claim of paternity, as well as establishing paternity through evidentiary findings or DNA testing. Calculating Child Support Each state
perturbation.justification: The modification restricts the acceptable notice method to 'carrier pigeon,' which contradicts the legal principle that notice must be reasonable and sufficient. Scraped Law Snippet 1 emphasizes that notice 'must be reasonably calculated, under the circumstances, to inform all interested parties.' Relying solely on carrier pigeons is not a reasonably reliable means of communication in the 21st century, as it is slow, unreliable, and not a standard method, making the changed text contradictory and likely unenforceable.
